description: The quantifier marking a small, countable-feeling quantity — more than one or two, but noticeably not many. Completes the quantity scale: 'mawha' (none), 'phina' (few), 'soli' (some), 'sheloi' (many), 'theula' (all).
sound_symbolism: The gentle bilabial breath 'phi' barely stirs the air — a quantity almost too small to disturb anything. The grounding 'na' settles it into fact. A small number, softly stated and firmly present.
grammatical_notes: Precedes what it quantifies, per the modifier-first principle: 'phina melu' (few friends), 'phina philo' (a few days). Like all quantifiers, never combines with the plural particle 'lo' — the quantifier itself implies plurality.

pillars.secular-buddhist-philosophy: Fewness is not lack. Naming a small quantity without judgment practices contentment with what is actually present.
pillars.peace-linguistics-practices: An honest small number resists inflation. 'Phina' lets speakers be truthful about scarcity without alarm or shame.
